public boolean isFascinating(int n) {

   if (3 * n > 999) return false;

   int twoN = 2 * n;

   int threeN = 3 * n;

   String s = Integer.toString(n) + Integer.toString(twoN) + Integer.toString(threeN);

   // Check for 0's

   for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {

       if (c == '0') return false;

   }

   // Check for uniqueness

   Set<Character> set = new HashSet<>();

   for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {

       set.add(c);

   }

   return set.size() == 9;

}

public boolean isFascinating(int n) {
    if (3 * n > 999) return false;
    String s = Integer.toString(n) + Integer.toString(2 * n) + Integer.toString(3 * n);
    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
        if (c == '0') return false;
    }
    Set<Character> set = new HashSet<>();
    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
        set.add(c);
    }
    return set.size() == 9;
}

But what about the time and space complexity?

Now, code in Java:

import java.util.Set;
import java.util.HashSet;

class Solution {
    public boolean isFascinating(int n) {
        if (3 * n > 999) return false;
        String s = Integer.toString(n) + Integer.toString(2 * n) + Integer.toString(3 * n);
        for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
            if (c == '0') return false;
        }
        Set<Character> set = new HashSet<>();
        for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
            set.add(c);
        }
        return set.size() == 9;
    }
}

return set.size() == 9;

    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
        if (c == '0') return false;
        set.add(c);
    }
    return set.size() == 9;
}

So code steps:

public boolean isFascinating(int n) {

   if (3 * n > 999) return false;

   int twoN = 2 * n;

   int threeN = 3 * n;

   String s = Integer.toString(n) + Integer.toString(twoN) + Integer.toString(threeN);

   // Check for 0's

   for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {

       if (c == '0') return false;

   }

   // Check for uniqueness

   Set<Character> set = new HashSet<>();

   for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {

       set.add(c);

   }

   return set.size() == 9;

}

But in Java, for the Set, we need to import java.util.Set; and java.util.HashSet;. But assuming that the code is written properly.

So the code for the method is:

public boolean isFascinating(int n) {
    if (3 * n > 999) return false;
    String s = Integer.toString(n) + Integer.toString(2 * n) + Integer.toString(3 * n);
    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
        if (c == '0') return false;
    }
    Set<Character> set = new HashSet<>();
    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
        set.add(c);
    }
    return set.size() == 9;
}
